title: Blood and Jade Sky
blurb: Blood stains the jade sky. A forgotten prince hides in plain ink. His brother’s ghost whispers through shattered glyphs. The city that should worship him is building his tomb.
comparable: For fans of The Poppy War by R.F. Kuang, The Shadow of the Wind by Carlos Ruiz Zafón

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The scriptorium’s air smelled of damp bark and aged limewash. Sunlight slanted through the high louvered vents, striping the glyph panels like the bars of a cage. T’ot’ ran calloused fingertips over the freshly carved stone, tracing the hidden chamber’s edges. His chisel had made it real now—one more secret laid into the temple’s ribs.

Junior Scribe Chak leaned over his shoulder, squinting. “Again with the obelisk?”

T’ot’ didn’t look up. “Master Itzel assigned it.”

Chak snorted. “Obelisk’s already carved. Twice. By masters.”

“Then it needs a third copy.”

Chak’s shadow stretched long across the bench. “You’re wasting stone. You know that, don’t you?”

T’ot’ lifted his mallet, tapped the chisel’s edge once—precise, deliberate. Stone shards skittered across the bench like fleeing insects. “Better to waste stone than time.”

Itzel’s voice cut across the room before Chak could retort. “T’ot’.”

T’ot’ didn’t move. He didn’t have to. The summon was for him.

He set the mallet down, brushed dust from his knees, and stood. The scriptorium’s silence followed him as he crossed the floor, sandals whispering on limestone. Itzel stood behind a low dais littered with bark codices and charcoal sketches. His face was all sharp angles beneath the fringe of a scribe’s headband.

“Another panel?” Itzel asked.

“Yes, Master.”

“For the obelisk.”

“Yes.”

Itzel exhaled through his nose. “You have a steady hand. That’s not in question.”

T’ot’ waited. He’d learned long ago that praise from Itzel was like a slow rain—it came, it soaked you, but you never knew when it would stop.

“But.” Itzel tapped a glyph sketch on the dais. “This is not the obelisk you carved last week. Or the week before.”

T’ot’ didn’t answer.

Itzel’s fingers hovered over the design—elaborate, symmetrical, the kind of thing a master would approve. “This one shows the chamber. The hidden chamber.”

T’ot’ kept his breathing even. “A copy must be exact.”

“Exact?” Itzel’s voice sharpened. “No copy would include the chamber’s dimensions. No master would. Unless they were trying to preserve something that should stay buried.”

T’ot’ met his gaze. “Master Itzel, I only follow orders.”

Itzel stared at him. Then, slowly, he shook his head. “You’re wasting your talent on ghosts.”

T’ot’ said nothing.

Itzel waved a hand. “Go. Before I decide your ‘talent’ is better spent hauling lime.”

T’ot’ bowed, turned, and walked back to his bench. Chak was already carving an eclipse glyph with unnecessary force, sending black flecks flying.

“Careful,” T’ot’ said. “You’ll chip the stone. And the calendar can’t have errors.”

Chak’s chisel slipped. A jagged fracture split the eclipse’s eye.

Chak’s face darkened. “Obsidian take you.”

T’ot’ picked up his own tools and began to work on the new panel, its surface still rough from yesterday’s roughing. He didn’t speak again.

The hours passed in the scriptorium’s quiet rhythm—ink being mixed, quills being trimmed, glyphs being debated. T’ot’ carved with the same rhythm, his hands moving like they belonged to someone else. He carved the hidden chamber’s entrance. He carved the false floor slab. He carved the narrow shaft that led down to the waiting chamber beneath the Bloodline Obelisk.

Outside, a bell chimed for the midday meal. Scribes rose, stretching, murmuring, filing toward the courtyard where baskets of maize cakes and gourds of chocolate waited. Chak tossed his mallet onto the bench and left without a word.

T’ot’ stayed.

Itzel had gone to oversee the junior scribes’ meal. The scriptorium was empty except for the wind sighing through the vents.

He reached beneath the bench and pulled out a small bundle wrapped in bark cloth. Inside was a single red quill, its tip still wet with ink. He’d taken it from the altar of the Maize God that morning, slipped it into his pouch without anyone noticing.

He set it on the bench.

It sat there, vibrant against the worn stone.

He turned back to his panel, picked up his chisel.

The quill remained still.

T’ot’ carved steadily, the sound of stone on stone filling the room like a heartbeat.

Then—

A drop of ink hit the floor.

T’ot’ froze. The chisel slipped, gouging a jagged line across the panel.

No one was there.

No one could have touched it.

The quill hadn’t moved.

He set the chisel down slowly. Looked at the floor. A single drop of crimson spread like a wound.

It hadn’t fallen from the quill.

It had risen from the floor itself.

Then another drop. Then another. A thin line of red began to crawl across the limestone, slow and deliberate, like a finger drawing a message.

The scriptorium’s silence pressed in.

T’ot’ didn’t breathe.

The drops moved toward the door. Then paused. Turned.

And began to rise—not toward the ceiling, but into the air, forming a single word in perfect glyphs:

*Run.*

Chapter 2

The Festival of the Maize God began at dawn with the first offering—a basket of yellow kernels placed at the temple’s base, its weight pressing down the sun’s edge just enough to make the air shimmer. T’ot’ knelt with the other scribes, pressing his forehead to the limestone steps, palms flat on stone. Around him, nobles in jade-spangled mantles murmured prayers, their voices rising in careful harmony. The scent of copal incense clung to his sleeves as he rose, careful not to let his sandals touch the sacred soil more than necessary.

Wak-K’in stood at the apex of the steps, his headdress of quetzal plumes catching the light like a blade. He raised a single hand, and the festival stilled.

“Let the maize grow tall,” he intoned, “let the harvest be plentiful. Let Tikal rise in strength through the wisdom of this calendar season.”

T’ot’ kept his eyes on the ground, fingers curled into his palm where no one could see.

A basket of ripe ears passed down the line. He took one, touched it to his forehead, then placed it on the next altar without a word.

Chak elbowed him. “You’re going to spoil the rhythm.”

“Better than stuttering through a lie,” T’ot’ muttered.

Chak’s face twitched. He moved away, joining the chorus of voices raised in praise. T’ot’ stayed at the back, where the shadows pooled deepest.

Then the music began—a deep drum, shakers of gourd, flutes weaving between. The nobles danced in spirals, their steps precise as calendar glyphs, their laughter sharp as obsidian blades. T’ot’ watched from the edge, tracing the patterns in the dust with his sandal.

Wak-K’in descended the steps, his movements deliberate, his gaze sweeping the crowd like a censor burning away impurities. Then he stopped. Right in front of T’ot’.

“Scribe T’ot’,” he said, smiling. “Stand forward.”

T’ot’ obeyed. The drumming faltered just a fraction.

“You’ve been carving eclipses,” Wak-K’in said. “Tell us—what is the next one?”

Heads turned. Scribes stiffened. Chak’s mouth opened, then clamped shut.

T’ot’ felt the weight of eyes pressing in. “The next solar eclipse,” he said, voice steady, “falls on the day 8 Ahau, 13 Ceh.”

Wak-K’in tilted his head. “And the time?”

“Mid-morning. The shadow will cross the sun at the hour of the serpent’s bite.”

The crowd shifted. A murmur rippled through the nobles.

Wak-K’in’s smile widened. “Remarkable. And yet—” He gestured to Lady Ti-Hu, who stepped forward, unfurling a bark codex. She laid it open on a low altar of cedar.

T’ot’ saw it at once. The eclipse glyph was wrong. The serpent’s head was misaligned. The date was off by three days.

The nobles gasped. Laughter burst from the back.

Wak-K’in spread his arms. “Our humble scribe speaks of an eclipse that will never come. Or perhaps he speaks of one that already did—hiding in the dark where the past belongs.”

T’ot’ didn’t flinch. But his fingers curled into his palm until the nails bit his skin.

Ahau-Batz’ stood near the edge of the circle, his arms crossed. His face was unreadable, but his knuckles were white. He took a step forward—then stopped, as if held by an unseen hand.

Wak-K’in turned to him. “Ahau-Batz’, esteemed astronomer. What say you to T’ot’s prediction?”

Ahau-Batz’ looked at the flawed glyph. Then at T’ot’.

“It’s not possible,” he said quietly. “The alignment is off. The cord is broken.”

The court erupted. Nobles clapped Wak-K’in on the back. A cup of chocolate spilled onto the stones, dark and glossy as blood.

T’ot’ stared at the ground. The shamed silence wrapped around him like a shroud.

Wak-K’in leaned close, his breath warm with cinnamon. “You are mistaken, scribe. As always.”

T’ot’ said nothing.

Wak-K’in clapped him on the shoulder—a touch too hard, a message to the crowd. “Perhaps you should spend less time carving fantasies and more time copying the calendar we *already* have.”

Nobles laughed. T’ot’ felt the floor tilt beneath him, just slightly.

Then Wak-K’in moved on, embracing another noble, his voice rising again in triumph. The festival resumed. The dancers spun. The drums pounded.

T’ot’ slipped away, melting into the shadows behind the temple’s eastern stair.

He didn’t stop until he reached the Black Cenote’s mouth—a narrow fissure in the bedrock, hidden behind a curtain of vines. The air inside was cool, damp, thick with the scent of wet limestone and old rain. He crouched at the edge, pulling a folded slip of bark from his pouch. On it, written in jagged glyphs, were the true eclipse calculations—corrected. The ones that would prove Wak-K’in’s system was flawed.

Footsteps echoed behind him.

T’ot’ didn’t turn. He knew the step, the breath, the silence that came before a presence.

“You saw it too,” Ahau-Batz’ said.

T’ot’ folded the bark carefully, tucked it back into his pouch. “I saw a mistake.”

Ahau-Batz’ stepped closer. “Not just a mistake. A lie. Built into the calendar itself.”

T’ot’ finally looked at him. His face was narrow, his eyes dark under the sweep of his hair. A scar ran from his temple to his jawline—mark of exile.

“And you said nothing,” T’ot’ said.

Ahau-Batz’ exhaled through his nose. “I couldn’t. Not in front of them.”

“Why now?”

“Because you just let Wak-K’in humiliate you in front of the court. And you didn’t fight back.”

T’ot’ turned to the cenote. The water below was black and still, reflecting nothing. “What would you have me do? Announce I’m not a scribe? That I’m the forgotten prince? That the calendar I carved is the one that saved Tikal—not Wak-K’in’s flawed stone?”

Ahau-Batz’ went quiet. Then, softly: “Who are you?”

T’ot’ closed his eyes. For a heartbeat, he imagined telling him. The truth, like a stone dropped into the cenote—no splash, just silence.

He opened them. “A man who knows the seasons.”

Ahau-Batz’ stepped even closer. Close enough that T’ot’ could feel the heat of him. “You’re shaking.”

T’ot’ hadn’t noticed. He flexed his hands.

“You’re afraid,” Ahau-Batz’ said.

“Aren’t you?”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t answer. His gaze dropped to T’ot’s lips.

T’ot’ looked away. “This isn’t wise.”

“Nothing is,” Ahau-Batz’ whispered. “Especially not love.”

A stone shifted behind them.

T’ot’ stiffened. He turned slowly.

No one was there.

The vines didn’t move.

The air was still.

But the cenote’s surface rippled—once, twice—as if something had brushed the water from below.

Ahau-Batz’ stepped back. “Did you see—”

T’ot’ raised a hand. Silence.

Then a whisper of movement in the shadows. A glint of light on metal.

T’ot’ lunged forward, grabbing Ahau-Batz’ arm and dragging him behind a jutting slab of rock. They pressed close, breathing hard.

A figure emerged from the foliage—tall, cloaked, moving with the quiet precision of a hunter. A spy. Wak-K’in’s eyes and ears in the dark.

T’ot’ held his breath. The spy paused, scanning the cenote’s edge. Then, with deliberate slowness, reached into his belt and withdrew a folded bark slip. He tucked it beneath a loose stone near the fissure’s mouth.

He didn’t look up. Didn’t linger.

He melted back into the jungle.

When he was gone, T’ot’ exhaled. His hands were still clenched.

Ahau-Batz’ pulled the slip free. Unfurled it. His face went pale.

T’ot’ didn’t need to read it to know. He could feel the weight of the words.

*By order of the Council, Scribe T’ot’ is to be taken into custody for questioning. No resistance will be tolerated.*

Ahau-Batz’ folded it back up. His jaw was tight.

T’ot’ met his gaze. “They know.”

Ahau-Batz’ nodded. “Not everything. But enough.”

A gust of wind rattled the vines. The cenote’s water darkened.

T’ot’ stood. “I have to go.”

Ahau-Batz’ caught his wrist. “Where?”

“Somewhere Wak-K’in won’t look.”

“And then what?”

T’ot’ looked at him—really looked. The scar, the fire in his eyes, the way his fingers still held T’ot’s wrist like a lifeline.

“Then,” T’ot’ said, “we do what we were never supposed to do.”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t let go. “We?”

T’ot’ didn’t answer. He pulled free and melted into the jungle, leaving Ahau-Batz’ standing in the shadow of the cenote, the arrest order burning in his hand.

Chapter 3

The scriptorium’s lower vault was a tomb of stone and dust, lit only by a single clay oil lamp that sputtered on its iron hook. T’ot’ crouched on the floor, his back against the cold limestone, the lamp’s flicker casting jagged shadows across the walls. Before him lay a slab of smoothed cedar, propped on a stack of bark codices. On it, he had etched the Bloodline Obelisk’s hidden chamber in careful, minute strokes—a map of memory.

He traced a finger along the curve of a glyph. His brother’s face appeared in the stone, carved in miniature, locked behind the obelisk’s sealed door. Chan-Bahlum’s eyes stared back at him, permanent as the sun.

A floorboard creaked above.

T’ot’ didn’t look up. He knew the step, the pause, the slow exhalation of someone used to moving quietly through the world. Ixchel.

She stood in the doorway, framed by the dark mouth of the stairwell, her basket of weaving tools clutched tight against her chest. The lamp’s glow caught the jade beads woven into her hair, turning them to green fire.

“You shouldn’t be down here,” she said.

T’ot’ dipped his reed brush into the ink pot. “I need to finish.”

“It’s not safe. Not after today.”

He inked the brush and drew a jagged line across the wood—an edge of the hidden chamber. “Wak-K’in’s spies won’t look for me where they expect me to hide.”

“And where’s that?”

“In plain sight.”

Ixchel set her basket down with a thud. The tools inside chimed like bells in a dead cave. She stepped forward, her sandals whispering against the stone. “You’re mapping the unmarked places. The places they erased.”

T’ot’ didn’t answer.

Ixchel knelt beside him, close enough that he could smell the thread on her sleeves, the faint scent of copal resin in her hair. She didn’t touch the slab. Didn’t ask to see.

Instead, she reached into the pouch at her waist and pulled out a torn scrap of royal tapestry. The edge was frayed, the embroidery half-looped, as if ripped from something larger. She held it between two fingers, letting it unfurl.

T’ot’s breath caught.

There, stitched in crimson and gold, was the royal jaguar—its tail looping into a glyph only he would recognize.

The *K’inich Ahau* glyph. The Sun-Faced Lord.

His name.

Ixchel folded it back into her palm. “You know what this is.”

T’ot’ set the brush down. The ink glistened. “Proof.”

“Proof you’ll get yourself killed for.”

“Better than proof they already have.”

She looked at him then, really looked—her dark eyes unreadable beneath the shadow of her hairline. “You think the past will save you?”

“I think it’s all that’s left.”

Ixchel tucked the scrap back into her pouch. She didn’t fasten the drawstrings. “Wak-K’in has men watching the scriptorium. Not just scribes. Guards. Hunters.”

T’ot’ picked up the brush again. “Then I’ll move faster.”

A silence settled between them, thick as the dust on the shelves. Above, the scriptorium groaned in the heat of midday.

Ixchel’s fingers twitched against her thigh. “There’s something else.”

T’ot’ paused. “Yes?”

She didn’t look at him. “Ahau-Batz’ was seen with you last night. At the cenote.”

T’ot’s chest tightened. “He was leaving. I was thinking.”

“Thinking what?”

He drew a long breath. “That he might be the only one who sees me and not the role I play.”

Ixchel exhaled slowly through her nose. “That’s dangerous talk.”

“So is standing in the dark holding secrets.”

She reached out, not to touch him, but to steady the edge of the slab. The cedar shivered. “You’re playing with fire.”

“No.” T’ot’ dipped the brush again. “I’m playing with ash.”

Ixchel stood abruptly, her skirt whispering against the stone. “I can’t give you the tapestry.”

T’ot’ looked up. “Why not?”

“Because if I do, you’ll run. And they’ll catch you. And your brother’s legacy will burn with you.”

“Or it will rise.”

“Or you’ll die before you ever see the throne.”

T’ot’ laid the brush across the slab. He met her gaze. “Then why keep it?”

Ixchel hesitated. Then, with deliberate slowness, she pulled the scrap from her pouch one last time. She held it out.

T’ot’s fingers hovered over it.

“Take it,” she said. “But don’t take it to the obelisk. Not yet.”

He folded it into his own pouch, pressing it close to his skin. “Where then?”

“Somewhere Wak-K’in’s eyes won’t go. Somewhere even the spies fear to tread.”

“Where?”

Ixchel’s lips parted. Shut. Then: “The Black Cenote.”

T’ot’ went still. “Why there?”

“Because it remembers everything. And it gives nothing back.”

He knew what she meant. The cenote’s water was black for a reason. It drank secrets. Never released them.

Ixchel turned toward the stair. “Be careful, little prince.”

The words hung in the air like dust motes.

T’ot’ didn’t correct her.

She climbed the stairs, her steps fading into the silence. The lamp flickered. Shadows stretched long and thin across the vault.

T’ot’ exhaled. He folded the map carefully, wrapped it in bark, and tucked it into his belt. Then he stood, brushing dust from his knees.

His satchel lay on a low shelf, half-unlaced.

He reached for it.

Something poked through the flap.

A strip of bark, folded small.

T’ot’ pulled it free.

Unfurled it.

The edict was short. Stark.

*By command of the High Architect, Scribe T’ot’ is to be taken into royal custody for treason against the calendar. All scribes and guardians are to comply.*

No signature. No seal. Only urgency.

No date. No place.

But the hand was familiar.

The same scribe who had written the false eclipse prophecy.

T’ot’ folded the bark back into itself. He tucked it into his satchel, careful not to crease it.

Footsteps echoed above. Not Ixchel’s. Heavier. Slower.

A spy.

T’ot’ blew out the lamp.

The vault plunged into darkness.

He listened to the steps descend, one careful step at a time.

The spy wasn’t coming down.

He was going up.

T’ot’ waited until the silence swallowed the last echo.

Then he moved.

Past the shelves. Past the hides of dead foxes used to dry ink. Past the bundles of quills tied in crimson thread.

He stopped at the far wall. Pressed his palm to a loose stone.

It shifted.

A hidden niche opened.

Inside lay a single obsidian blade, its edge still sharp from the last scribe who had needed it.

T’ot’ took it.

Then he turned.

The spy was still above him.

The door to the vault was open.

No one stood in it.

But on the floor, just inside the threshold, lay a single feather.

Pale. Green.

A quetzal plume.

T’ot’ stepped into the corridor.

The feather didn’t move.

But the air did.

It shifted, just for a heartbeat, as though something unseen had exhaled behind him.

Then it was still again.

T’ot’ walked forward.

Toward the light.

Toward the waiting world.

Chapter 4

The Great Plaza of Tikal burned under the midday sun. Limestone dust rose in clouds as the gathered court fanned themselves with woven mats, their polished jade ornaments glinting like scattered water. At the center, the Great Twin Pyramid loomed—its twin peaks slicing the sky, their steps lined with freshly painted stucco that smelled of rain-washed earth.

Lord Wak-K’in stood on the lower platform, his crimson cloak spread wide like the wings of some great bird of prey. His hands rested on the polished cedar table before him, fingers splayed over the new calendar stone—a massive slab carved with glyphs that glowed blue in the reflected light.

“A gift to the gods and to Tikal,” he called out, voice carrying over the square. “A system that does not lie. That does not guess. That *knows.*”

The crowd roared. Priests beat drums. Children scrambled over temple steps to see. High Priest Kinich lifted his obsidian mirror and reflected the sun toward the stone, and for a heartbeat the glyphs burned white-hot.

Wak-K’in smiled. It did not reach his eyes.

In the shadow of the pyramid’s western flank, K’inich—disguised as a lowly scribe with ink-stained fingers and a bent back—watched from the edge of the press-ganged laborers. He held a fresh pot of pigment, his body angled to avoid notice. The weight of the stolen tapestry scrap burned against his thigh.

A gust of wind carried the scent of copal and sweat. He exhaled through his teeth and shifted his weight.

Wak-K’in’s voice dropped, intimate as a blade sliding between ribs. “This cycle, the gods have named the truth for us. The days are not falling. The sky is not breaking. The blame lies not in the heavens, but in those who would hide the sky within their own hands.”

Murmurs rippled through the crowd. A noblewoman dropped her fan. It shattered on the stones.

K’inich’s gaze flicked to the edge of the platform. There, half-hidden behind a pillar, Ahau-Batz’ stood with arms crossed, his astronomer’s staff resting against his shoulder. He looked bored. Unimpressed.

Wak-K’in raised his hands. The chanting swelled.

“To the New Sun! To the One Who Speaks the Sky!”

The crowd roared again.

A man in fine robes stepped forward—Advisor Xam, his face a map of careful loyalty. He bowed low. “Great Lord, the people are moved beyond measure. But… there is one question.”

Wak-K’in tilted his head. “Speak.”

“Your calculations for the coming eclipse… they show no shadow. No rupture in the sky.”

The smile on Wak-K’in’s lips twitched. “Is that a question?”

“A concern. The people expect to see the gods consume the sun. Without it… doubt may take root.”

Wak-K’in’s fingers curled into his palms. The skin over his knuckles whitened. “The gods do not perform for the crowd.”

Xam bowed again. “Of course. But the calendar is meant to guide the people. Not just the priests.”

A hush fell. The drummers had stopped.

Wak-K’in stepped closer to the edge of the platform. His shadow stretched long and thin across the stones. “The eclipse will come as marked,” he said softly. “And when it does, the people will see the truth.”

K’inich felt the words like a stone in his gut.

Truth.

He knew the truth.

He had rewritten the eclipse.

He had stolen the sky’s alignment from Wak-K’in’s hands and set it back where it belonged. A small correction. A whisper of time. But one that would unravel the architect’s perfect system when the gods finally moved.

Xam hesitated. Then, with a practiced bow: “As you command, Great Timekeeper.”

Wak-K’in turned away, his cloak rippling. Kinich followed, murmuring in his ear.

K’inich exhaled. He bent down, pretended to adjust his sandal. When he rose, he caught Ahau-Batz’ looking at him.

A shift in the shoulders. A lift of the chin.

A question.

K’inich dipped his chin toward the pyramid’s shadow. A silent invitation.

He didn’t wait for an answer.

He turned and melted into the press of bodies, his pot of pigment held low. The heat pressed against his back like a hand.

He ducked into the narrow corridor between the temple base and the royal storehouse. The stones here were warm, sun-baked. The air smelled of old corn and damp lime.

Ahau-Batz’ appeared behind him, silent as a shadow. He didn’t speak. Just stepped into the light.

K’inich set the pot down. Leaned against the wall. His fingers brushed the pouch at his waist.

“You were there,” he said quietly. “At the cenote.”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t deny it. “So were you.”

“Why?”

“Because you’re the only one who looks at me and sees more than a disgraced name.”

K’inich looked away. “Or less.”

Ahau-Batz’ stepped closer. The scent of chaya and roasted squash clung to his sleeves—his dinner, carried from the royal kitchens. “I read the eclipse prediction. It was wrong.”

K’inich didn’t answer.

Ahau-Batz’ lowered his voice. “You fixed it.”

Silence.

K’inich exhaled through his nose. “What do you want, Ahau-Batz’?”

“I want to know why a scribe with ink on his fingers cares about the sky’s mistakes.”

“I care about the sky being wrong.”

“That’s not an answer.”

“It’s the only one I’ll give.”

Ahau-Batz’ stared at him. Then, without warning, he reached out and touched K’inich’s wrist. Not a grip. A brush. Almost accidental.

K’inich didn’t pull away.

The gesture settled between them like a shared secret.

Ahau-Batz’ leaned in. “I’ve seen the obelisk’s defacement,” he said. “I know what’s hidden behind it. I know what you’re looking for.”

K’inich’s pulse jumped. “You don’t know anything.”

“I know a prince doesn’t hide in plain sight unless he’s desperate.”

K’inich turned his head. Met Ahau-Batz’ gaze. “Why tell me this?”

“Because I want to help.”

“Help?” K’inich laughed, low and rough. “You want to help a traitor?”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t flinch. “You’re not a traitor. You’re the one who saved the sky. That makes you the only honest man in this city.”

K’inich looked down at their hands—close now, fingers nearly touching.

A cough echoed in the corridor.

Both men stiffened.

Advisor Xam stood at the far end, his arms crossed, his dark eyes sharp beneath the brim of his wide hat. “Scribe T’ot’,” he called. “The High Architect requests your presence.”

K’inich’s blood turned to ice.

Ahau-Batz’ went still.

K’inich straightened. “Why me?”

Xam smiled. “Your glyphs are the finest in the scriptorium. He wishes to honor your work.”

K’inich forced a nod. “Of course.”

He stepped forward, careful to keep his face down. Ahau-Batz’ didn’t move. Didn’t speak.

Xam turned, leading the way through the sun-baked corridors.

K’inich walked behind, his sandals whispering on stone.

He didn’t look back.

But he felt Ahau-Batz’ eyes on him like a brand.

And for the first time in years, he wished he could trust someone.

They reached the private chamber where Wak-K’in held court behind curtains of woven cane. The air smelled of burning copal and polished wood.

Xam gestured K’inich inside.

K’inich stepped through.

The chamber was small. A single oil lamp burned on a low table. Wak-K’in sat on a reed mat, his back to the doorway. The advisor, High Priest Kinich, knelt beside him, whispering in his ear.

K’inich bowed low. “Great Lord, you summoned me?”

Wak-K’in didn’t turn. “Scribe T’ot’,” he said, voice smooth as wet clay. “Come. Stand with us.”

K’inich obeyed. His shadow stretched long across the stone.

Wak-K’in finally turned. His gaze was heavy, assessing. “Your eclipse glyphs,” he said. “They carry a weight.”

K’inich kept his face calm. “The gods demand precision.”

“Do they?” Wak-K’in reached out. Picked up a small tablet from the mat beside him. Turned it over. “Yet the people whisper that the sky does not move when I command it.”

K’inich didn’t blink. “The sky moves as it always has.”

Wak-K’in studied him. Then, slowly, he set the tablet down. “You are mistaken, scribe.”

K’inich said nothing.

Wak-K’in exhaled. “But I find you useful. Your hand is steady. Your eye is true.”

K’inich bowed again. “I live to serve.”

Wak-K’in nodded. “Good. Then you will serve by writing the dedication for the Twin Pyramid. The words must endure.”

K’inich dipped his head. “As you wish.”

Wak-K’in leaned back. The lamp flickered. Shadows danced across his face. “You know, T’ot’, I once believed the past was a chain. Something to be broken. Cast aside.”

K’inich waited.

“But now I see,” Wak-K’in said softly, “even chains can be repurposed. Made stronger. Made immortal.”

K’inich’s fingers twitched.

Wak-K’in was still looking at him. Really looking.

K’inich dipped his chin lower.

The silence grew thick.

Then Wak-K’in turned away. “Leave us.”

K’inich backed out, bowing, heart hammering.

He stepped into the corridor.

Xam was gone. The path was clear.

He turned.

And froze.

Xam stood ten paces away.

Holding a glyph tablet.

Engraved with a single name.

*T’ot’.*

Not erased.

Burned away.

Xam held it between two fingers. The light caught the scorched edges.

And then, slowly, deliberately, Xam turned the tablet over.

K’inich saw the other side.

A jagged hole.

A name erased from the record.

And a single line beneath.

*“K’inich Ahau-T’ot’: Dead by decree.”*

Xam’s thumb hovered over the flame of the nearest lamp.

K’inich’s breath caught.

Xam looked at him.

Smiled.

And flicked his wrist.

The tablet burst into flame.

K’inich lunged.

Chapter 5

The morning sun carved sharp edges into the Great Twin Pyramid’s eastern stairway. Limestone dust hung in the air like suspended ash, filtering the light into gold and gray streaks. Below, laborers strained under baskets of cut stone, their backs bent under the weight of Wak-K’in’s vision.

Ahau-Batz’ stood at the base of the northern pyramid, his staff planted in the ground like a weapon. The cedar wood glistened with sweat from his palms. Before him, Junior Architect Chik’in adjusted a plumb line with trembling hands.

K’inich crouched near the eastern stairway, pretending to repair a chipped glyph on the riser. His fingers traced the ancient carving—a jaguar paw, worn smooth by time. He wasn’t here to write. He was here to watch.

Wak-K’in’s voice cut through the noise like a knife. “Ahau-Batz’, your calculations are untimely.”

The astronomer didn’t look up from the plumb line. “They are precise, Great Lord.”

“Then explain the wobble.”

A pause. Chik’in’s hands stilled.

Ahau-Batz’ stepped back, wiping his palms on his woven kilt. “The northern pyramid’s base is offset by two finger-widths.”

Wak-K’in’s cloak flared as he turned, the crimson fabric snapping against the air. “Impossible.”

“Not impossible,” Ahau-Batz’ said quietly. “Unintentional.”

The laborers had stopped. They watched from the corners of their eyes, backs still bent but ears turned.

Wak-K’in’s jaw tightened. He strode toward the pyramid’s base, where the stones met the earth. He pressed a palm against the limestone. Then another.

The pyramid didn’t move.

But K’inich saw the shift—a tiny tremor in the vertical line of the upper stair. A hesitation in the stone’s weight.

Wak-K’in dropped his hand. “You are tired. The sun is unforgiving.”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t flinch. “I have checked the alignments three times. The western pyramid’s angle is correct. The northern is off.”

Wak-K’in’s voice dropped. “You dare accuse my design?”

“I accuse no one,” Ahau-Batz’ said. “But the stone does not lie.”

Chik’in stepped forward, his voice high and thin. “Great Architect, the northern stair—”

Wak-K’in silenced him with a glare. The junior architect swallowed hard and retreated.

Wak-K’in turned back to Ahau-Batz’. “Show me.”

Ahau-Batz’ led him to the plumb line, its weighted end swinging gently. He adjusted the string, stepped aside.

Wak-K’in followed the line with his eyes, then glanced at the pyramid’s peak. His fingers dug into his palms.

K’inich watched from the shadows. He knew Ahau-Batz’ wasn’t wrong. He had seen this before—in the crumbling ruins of Dos Pilas, where misaligned stones had toppled a dynasty. The knowledge had been passed down like a curse, from father to son.

Wak-K’in’s breath came fast. “This is a temporary settling. Common in new construction.”

“It is more than that,” Ahau-Batz’ said. “The weight shifts unevenly. The stress fractures will widen.”

Wak-K’in’s lips curled. “You speak like a man who fears collapse.”

“I speak like a man who has seen it happen.”

Silence settled over the plaza. The laborers stopped moving. Even the wind seemed to hold its breath.

Wak-K’in stepped closer to Ahau-Batz’, his shadow swallowing the astronomer whole. “You forget your place,” he said softly. “You are here by my grace. Not by blood. Not by right.”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t lower his gaze. “I forget nothing.”

Wak-K’in’s fingers twitched. For a heartbeat, K’inich thought he would strike him. But the High Architect only exhaled through his nose, then turned away.

“Recalculate,” he ordered over his shoulder. “Today. With every priest and scribe in the city watching.”

Ahau-Batz’ watched him go. Then he bent down and picked up a loose stone. He turned it in his hands, examining the edges.

Chik’in leaned in. “Master, he’ll have you branded a saboteur.”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t answer. He tossed the stone aside and wiped his hands again. His knuckles were white.

K’inich emerged from the shadows. He didn’t speak. He didn’t need to. The weight of what was coming hung between them like a stone slab.

Ahau-Batz’ looked at him. “You see it too.”

K’inich nodded toward the pyramid. “The stone is wrong.”

“Not just the stone,” Ahau-Batz’ said. “The whole system. The angles. The calculations. Wak-K’in didn’t build this to last. He built it to impress.”

K’inich’s fingers curled into fists. “And when it fails?”

Ahau-Batz’ met his gaze. “The city falls with it.”

They stood in silence as the laborers slowly resumed their work. The plumb line swung. The pyramid loomed.

Then Chik’in stepped between them, his voice a whisper. “They’re coming back.”

Wak-K’in returned with Advisor Xam and a knot of priests, their jade beads clattering like bones. They fanned out around Ahau-Batz’, cutting him off from K’inich.

Wak-K’in didn’t look at the pyramid. He looked at Ahau-Batz’. “Begin.”

Ahau-Batz’ bowed his head. “As you command.”

He moved to the base of the pyramid, where the first course of stones waited. The priests formed a semicircle around him, their eyes sharp and unblinking. Xam held a bundle of bark paper, a stylus poised over it.

K’inich watched from the edge of the press of laborers. He couldn’t hear the words, but he saw Ahau-Batz’ hands move—measuring, adjusting, correcting. The priests scribbled furiously. Xam’s stylus scratched like a rat in the dark.

Wak-K’in stood with his arms crossed, his gaze locked on Ahau-Batz’ like a predator watching prey.

Then Ahau-Batz’ straightened. He turned to Wak-K’in. “The alignment is flawed.”

Wak-K’in didn’t blink. “Show me.”

Ahau-Batz’ pointed to the upper stair. “See the gap between the third and fourth riser? The northern side is two fingers lower than the southern.”

Wak-K’in strode forward. He climbed the first few steps, testing each one with his foot. The pyramid groaned faintly under his weight, a sound like a held breath.

He reached the third riser. Paused.

Then he stepped up to the fourth.

The stone shifted.

A crack split the air. Thin. Sharp. Then another.

Wak-K’in froze.

The crack widened.

Laborers screamed. Priests scrambled back. Xam dropped his stylus and clawed at the air like he could catch the falling sky.

The northern pyramid trembled.

Dust rained down from the upper slopes.

K’inich’s heart hammered in his throat.

The pyramid wasn’t falling.

But it was *moving.*

Chik’in’s voice cut through the chaos, high and raw. “This is how the old pyramid at Dos Pilas fell…”

The words hung in the air like a curse.

Wak-K’in’s face went slack. His eyes locked onto the widening crack. His breath came fast and shallow.

Then he turned.

His gaze found K’inich.

And for the first time, there was no calculation in it.

Only fury.

Chapter 6

The air inside the Black Cenote smelled of wet stone and old reeds. T’ot’ crouched at the water’s edge, knees pressing into slick limestone. His breath fogged in the cool dim. Above, stars blinked through the circular opening, but down here, the dark was absolute.

A rustle.

He turned.

A figure emerged from the mist, cloaked in woven reeds, face hidden behind a jaguar-tooth mask. The exile moved soundlessly, stepping over slick boulders like they were dry land.

T’ot’ didn’t speak. He held out a bundle of obsidian blades—stolen from the scriptorium’s ceremonial cache. The exile reached into the folds of his cloak and withdrew a rolled packet of bark paper, tied with frayed maguey fiber.

No words exchanged. No names spoken.

The trade completed, the exile retreated the same way he came, vanishing into the mist without a ripple.

T’ot’ unfurled the chart.

Starlight caught the ink—precise glyphs, diagrams of planetary orbits, eclipse cycles. His fingers traced the curves. This was no ordinary chart. It was the private work of Ahau-Batz’’s father, the disgraced astronomer of Dos Pilas. The man Wak-K’in had exiled for questioning the Twin Pyramid’s alignment.

And it revealed the flaw.

Not a small crack. Not a settling. A fundamental miscalculation in the pyramid’s core geometry. The architect had built time itself into the stone—and got the angles wrong.

T’ot’’s pulse quickened. If he presented this to the scribes, to the court, Wak-K’in’s monument would collapse before the next katun cycle. Before the city’s celebration. Before the world could forget.

The water in the cenote rippled.

Not from the exile’s retreat. From behind him.

A shape emerged from the shadows—Ahau-Batz’ himself. He stood tall, his astronomer’s staff in one hand, his face half-lit by a flicker of torchlight bouncing off the water.

T’ot’ didn’t hide the chart. Didn’t flinch.

Ahau-Batz’ stepped closer, his sandals making no sound on the wet stone. “You’re taking risks.”

T’ot’ rolled the chart tight. “Not enough.”

“You stole those blades.”

“They weren’t sacred.”

“They were accounted for.”

T’ot’ met his gaze. “So is your father’s work. And yet it’s buried in a cenote, not on a pyramid wall.”

Ahau-Batz’ exhaled through his nose. His fingers flexed around the staff. “You’re playing a dangerous game.”

“So are you.”

A beat.

Then Ahau-Batz’ knelt beside him, close enough that T’ot’ felt the heat of his body. Close enough to smell the cedar smoke still clinging to his skin. Close enough to hear the quiet rhythm of his breath.

“Show me,” Ahau-Batz’ said.

T’ot’ hesitated. Then he unrolled the chart just enough for the key diagram to catch the dim light.

Ahau-Batz’ leaned in.

His breath hitched.

For a moment, they were two boys again—no titles, no bloodlines, just the quiet thrill of discovery under the night sky. The kind of moment T’ot’ had almost forgotten existed.

Then Ahau-Batz’ stiffened.

“This isn’t possible,” he whispered.

T’ot’ didn’t answer. He traced the misaligned obliquity lines with his fingertip. The error was deliberate. Not an accident. Wak-K’in had altered the sacred angles to force an eclipse prediction that would never come.

Ahau-Batz’ looked at him, eyes sharp in the dark. “You knew.”

Not a question.

T’ot’ kept his voice low. “I know the sky.”

“And the sky doesn’t lie.”

“No. But men do.”

Ahau-Batz’ reached out. His fingers brushed T’ot’'s wrist—not possessive, not urgent. Just contact. Grounding.

T’ot’ didn’t pull away.

“What are you going to do?” Ahau-Batz’ asked.

“Use it.”

“To do what?”

“Undo him.”

Ahau-Batz’ exhaled. “You’ll start a war.”

“He already started it. When he built a lie into stone.”

A slow nod. Acceptance.

Then Ahau-Batz’ reached into his own cloak and pulled out a second chart—smaller, more worn. The edges frayed from use. He unfolded it carefully.

T’ot’ recognized it instantly. His own work. The stolen pages from the *Tzolk’in Haab’*, the codex that Wak-K’in had erased and claimed as his own.

“You kept it,” T’ot’ said.

“You hid it,” Ahau-Batz’ replied.

They sat side by side in the cenote’s breathless dark, two boys who had become something else—something sharper, something braver. The torchlight guttered. The water lapped.

Then—

A sound from above.

A footstep on wet limestone.

Both men froze.

Ahau-Batz’ stood quickly, staff ready. T’ot’ rolled the charts and stuffed them into his kilt’s hidden pouch.

A shadow passed over the cenote’s opening.

Not a person. A bird. An owl, silent, wings wide against the stars.

T’ot’ exhaled.

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t relax. He crouched again, listening.

Footsteps. More than one.

Torchlight flickered at the rim of the cenote.

T’ot’ grabbed Ahau-Batz’ arm. Pulled him back into the deeper shadows.

They waited.

The voices came down—low, urgent.

“—patrol found fresh footprints at the eastern edge.”

“Concealed? Or routed?”

“Masked. Not ours.”

A laugh. Cold. Calculating.

T’ot’ recognized the voice.

Lady Ti-Hu.

The spymaster had found them.

Before Ahau-Batz’ could react, T’ot’ yanked him into the water.

They sank beneath the surface, the current pulling them deeper into the cenote’s hidden tunnels. The torchlight above grew distant. Voices echoed, then faded.

T’ot’ surfaced in a narrow passage, gasping. Ahau-Batz’ emerged beside him, dripping, staff slick in his grip.

No time to speak.

They moved.

The tunnel twisted. The air grew thick with the scent of wet earth and old death. Bones glinted in the torchlight—remains of exiles, forgotten souls.

At the end of the passage, a narrow crevice opened into blackness.

T’ot’ hesitated. “This leads to the scriptorium’s underground drain.”

Ahau-Batz’ nodded. “Safer than the cenote mouth.”

They slipped through.

Behind them, torchlight flickered at the passage’s entrance.

A voice hissed.

“The architect’s arrogance is his tomb.”

Then—silence.

The torchlight vanished.

The crevice sealed itself in darkness.

T’ot’ pressed forward, fingers trailing the damp stone.

They were inside.

But the net had tightened.

Chapter 7

The Royal Scriptorium smelled of damp bark, crushed corn paste, and the sharp tang of limestone dust. Sunlight slanted through high lattices, painting the floor in gold bars that moved too slowly to be real. K’inich stood at the back, arms crossed, watching the flicker of quills on bark as scribes labored over the day’s decrees. The clay pots of ink sat in neat rows, their lids tight. The air hummed with the scratch of reed pens, the murmur of voices, the occasional cough.

Scribe Master Itzel cleared his throat from the dais. His bald head gleamed like polished jade. “Today’s reading,” he began, voice steady, “from the Council of Time, Decree Twelve—on the alignment of the Twin Pyramid’s western stair.”

A scribe unrolled a bark sheet. Glyphs unfurled like a snake waking from sleep.

Itzel droned through the first line. Then paused.

He blinked at the glyphs.

Something shifted in his posture—shoulders tensing, fingers tightening around the bark. His voice wavered. “The stair shall stand…” He hesitated. “…as proof of Wak-K’in’s vision.”

A cough from the back row.

Itzel’s eyes flicked toward the sound. His throat worked. He tried again. “The stair shall…”

He stopped.

A ripple passed through the chamber. Scribes glanced at each other. Someone shifted a pot of ink. Its lid rattled.

Itzel’s voice dropped to a mutter. “The old prince’s name… it’s here.”

A hush.

Then, quickly, “—no. No, that’s not—” His throat clicked. He coughed again, louder this time, as if to swallow the words. “The stair shall stand… as the sky stands firm.”

Silence.

K’inich felt the weight of every gaze sliding away from Itzel, skimming over the scribes, and finally landing on him. He remained still, face a mask of humble attention. But inside, his pulse thudded against his ribs.

Itzel’s hands trembled as he rolled the bark back up. “The next decree,” he said, voice too bright. “From the Obsidian Cache—”

A clay pot fell.

It shattered on the stone floor.

The sound cracked through the chamber like a thunderclap.

Every scribe flinched. One screamed.

Itzel’s face paled. “Curse it,” he hissed. “That was the winter solstice pot—”

The noise didn’t fade. It echoed—from the walls, the pillars, the very air—like a drum beaten beneath the floorboards.

Then it stopped.

Too late.

Gasps. Murmurs. A scribe clutched her throat.

K’inich didn’t move. But his fingers dug into his upper arms, nails biting into skin through the rough-spun cotton.

Itzel wiped his brow with the back of his hand. “A bad omen,” he said weakly. “Nothing more.”

A young scribe, barely more than a novice, pointed at the broken shards. “The pot was already cracked,” she said. “I saw it this morning.”

Itzel stared at her. “You did?”

She nodded, face pale. “It had a hairline fracture. I was going to report it.”

A murmur rippled. People reached for omens in the shards—angles, cracks, imperfections that spelled doom or blessing.

K’inich exhaled slowly. The sound of the break still sang in his ears.

Then—footsteps.

Heavy. Measured.

The scribes at the front of the chamber stiffened. Even Itzel turned, his expression shifting from fear to forced calm.

Lord Wak-K’in stood in the doorway, framed by sunlight. His turquoise mosaic mask glinted, his cloak of macaw feathers swayed like a living thing. He filled the space with his presence, as if the room had been waiting for him.

“Scribe Master,” Wak-K’in said, voice smooth as polished obsidian. “What is this disturbance?”

Itzel bowed deeply. “Forgive the noise, High Architect. An accident. A pot fell.”

Wak-K’in stepped forward, his sandals clicking against stone. “A pot. Of what?”

“Solstice ink,” Itzel said quickly. “Old. Unused.”

“Unused?” Wak-K’in’s voice coiled. “Then why keep it?”

A scribe at the back raised a hand. “Respectfully, sir, it was the winter pot from the archives. We were… verifying its contents.”

Wak-K’in’s eyes flicked toward the fragments. Then to K’inich.

The room held its breath.

K’inich met the gaze. No flicker of recognition. No suspicion in those dark, calculating depths. Only curiosity—amused, assessing.

“A waste,” Wak-K’in said. “Of clay. Of ink. Of scribal focus.”

A cough cut through the silence.

Ahau-Batz’ stood near the back, leaning against a pillar. His hands were wrapped in strips of cloth, pale against the dark fibers. Blood seeped through in places, dark as old wine.

Wak-K’in turned. “Ahau-Batz’. The disgraced heir. Present at such a moment.” His voice dripped with contempt.

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t bow. Didn’t flinch. “I came to deliver a chart. The alignment of Saturn’s retrograde path.” His voice was calm, but the bandages on his hands twitched. “Though I see the Twin Pyramid doesn’t need precise calculations.”

Wak-K’in’s mask did not change, but the air around him seemed to darken. “Your father’s work was flawed. You know this.”

“As does the sky,” Ahau-Batz’ replied.

A ripple of shock passed through the scribes.

Wak-K’in stepped closer to Ahau-Batz’. “Careful, boy. You walk a razor’s edge between exile and obscurity.”

Ahau-Batz’ met his gaze. “I walk where the stars lead me.”

Silence.

Then Wak-K’in laughed—a dry, humorless sound. “Then I suggest you watch your step. The stars, like men, can mislead.”

He turned back to Itzel. “Finish the decrees. And clean that mess.”

Then he strode from the chamber, cloak swirling behind him like a storm cloud.

The moment he was gone, the scribes exhaled.

Itzel wiped his face again. “Back to work,” he said weakly. “Back to work.”

Ahau-Batz’ pushed off the pillar. He walked toward the back, where K’inich stood. His bandaged hands flexed.

K’inich didn’t speak.

Ahau-Batz’ stopped inches away. Close enough that K’inich smelled cedar smoke and sweat beneath the scent of blood.

“You saw Itzel’s mistake,” Ahau-Batz’ said quietly.

K’inich nodded.

“He almost said your name.”

K’inich kept his voice low. “Or Chan-Bahlum’s.”

Ahau-Batz’ flinched.

K’inich exhaled. “Wak-K’in knows something is wrong. Not who. Not yet. But the shard cracked the silence.”

Ahau-Batz’ stared at the broken pot. Shards glittered like scattered stars.

Then, from outside the scriptorium, a child’s voice rose—clear, surprised, ringing with the clarity of a bell.

“The scribe who wasn’t there saved us once!”

A scream. A scuffle. Boots on stone.

Ahau-Batz’ and K’inich both turned.

Through the lattice, they saw a small figure—barefoot, dark-haired—being dragged away by two guards. The child kicked, arms flailing. One guard clamped a hand over the boy’s mouth.

The words echoed in the chamber: “saved us once.”

Then the child vanished around a corner.

The scriptorium fell silent again.

K’inich’s chest tightened. He thought of the cenote. Of the tunnels. Of the hidden chambers beneath the pyramid.

Wak-K’in’s voice slithered back into his mind: The stars can mislead.

He looked at Ahau-Batz’.

The astronomer’s face was tight, his jaw set. His bandaged hands were clenched.

“They’re searching,” K’inich said.

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t answer. But his eyes burned.

Then, from the hallway, a new sound—distant, rhythmic. A drum.

Low.

Persistent.

Like a heartbeat.

Chapter 8

The Great Twin Pyramid rose like a clenched fist against the sky, its plasters still wet in patches, the scent of fresh stucco sharp under the midday sun. Below, the plaza teemed with laborers, priests, and scribes—each carrying baskets of lime, armfuls of carved stones, or stacks of bark paper. The central staircase had split the night before, a jagged crack splitting the limestone steps from top to bottom, wide enough to hide a fist.

K’inich stood at the edge of the plaza, squinting up at the fault line. His quill had been slick with ink when he’d drawn the corrected alignment on his bark sheet the night before. Now the pyramid groaned under Wak-K’in’s misplaced pride.

A scribe beside him sighed. “This is the third repair in a moon. The mortar won’t hold.”

“It wasn’t built to last,” K’inich said quietly.

The scribe shot him a look but said nothing.

A hand clapped K’inich’s shoulder.

He turned.

Ahau-Batz’ stood there, face flushed, his wrap of bloodied cloth around one hand still damp. His other arm cradled a rolled bark sheet, its edges frayed.

“You’re bleeding,” K’inich said.

Ahau-Batz’ flexed his fingers. “Not enough.”

K’inich stepped closer. “What happened?”

“Argued with the pyramid,” Ahau-Batz’ said. “It won the first round.”

K’inich reached out without thinking, brushing the bandage with his fingers. Ahau-Batz’ didn’t pull away.

“You shouldn’t provoke him.”

“He provoked me first.” Ahau-Batz’’s voice was low, controlled. “Called my father’s work a lie in front of the scribes.”

K’inich exhaled through his nose. “He wants the fault to spread.”

“Then let it.”

A cart groaned past, pulled by two slaves, carrying a load of jaguar pelts. The stench of blood mixed with lime.

K’inich lowered his voice. “The eclipse calculation isn’t the only flaw. The foundation stones are set on sand.”

Ahau-Batz’’s eyes darkened. “Wak-K’in knows.”

“I don’t think he does. Not fully. He thinks the pyramid is flawed because the math was wrong. But the math wasn’t wrong—it was sabotaged. The alignment was never meant to bear this weight.”

Ahau-Batz’ stared at the crack. “So the pyramid is bleeding.”

K’inich didn’t answer.

A commotion cut through the plaza.

Lady Ti-Hu’s spy moved like a shadow between the stalls, a young girl in tattered green, her dark braid bouncing as she slipped past merchants and priests. She paused at a fruit vendor, bought a mango, peeled it with quick fingers, and ate it while scanning the crowd.

K’inich tensed.

Ahau-Batz’ followed his gaze. “What?”

“She’s listening.”

“All spies listen.”

“Not like that. She’s planting something.”

Ahau-Batz’ shifted the bark under his arm. “What now?”

Before K’inich could answer, the girl flicked the mango pit aside and pressed something into the vendor’s hand—a coin, maybe. Then she turned and wove through the crowd toward the scriptorium gate.

K’inich moved.

Ahau-Batz’ fell into step beside him. “You’re not supposed to be out here.”

“I’m not.”

They followed at a distance, staying near stalls, keeping to the edges of conversation. The scriptorium’s high wall loomed ahead, its white stucco blinding in the sun.

Inside the gate, scribes milled, carrying pots of ink, rolls of bark, bundles of reeds. The girl ducked beneath their legs, slipping into the shadow of the storage hut.

K’inich and Ahau-Batz’ slipped in after her.

They found her crouched beside a stack of bark cylinders, prying at a loose stone in the wall. Behind it, a small pouch of feathers and a folded bark sheet glistened in the dim light.

She froze when she saw them.

Ahau-Batz’’s hand shot out, grabbing her wrist.

The girl—no older than ten—twisted, but Ahau-Batz’ held firm. She spat at his feet.

K’inich crouched beside them. “What’s in the pouch?”

The girl said nothing.

Ahau-Batz’ unwrapped her fingers, one by one. Her palm was scarred, the skin rough.

K’inich reached for the pouch.

She snatched it first and hurled it at Ahau-Batz’ chest.

Bark paper fluttered to the ground.

Ahau-Batz’ caught it midair.

He unfolded it.

His face went still.

K’inich leaned in.

It was a glyph. A half-rendered profile of a young man with crossed bones beneath his jaw—the mark of the Ajaw Kan. A prince. A lost heir.

K’inich’s breath stopped.

Ahau-Batz’ exhaled sharply. “Where did you get this?”

The girl smirked. “From the Blood Obelisk. They carved it again. Under the stone.”

K’inich’s pulse roared in his ears.

Ahau-Batz’’s grip tightened on the girl’s wrist. “Who told you to do this?”

“No one.” Her voice was flat. “The stone speaks at night. It sings.”

K’inich’s mind raced. The Blood Obelisk was defaced. But beneath—yes. The royal glyphs were still there, carved into the living rock, hidden by centuries of plaster and time.

Ahau-Batz’ stood abruptly, dragging the girl with him. “We need to take her to the scriptorium. To Itzel.”

The girl struggled. “Let me go! They’ll see me! They’ll know!”

K’inich grabbed her other arm. “Who’ll know?”

“The scribes. The guards. The High Architect.” Tears cut tracks through the dust on her cheeks. “They’re looking for you. The quiet scribe. The one who fixes things the sky forgot.”

K’inich’s blood turned to ice.

Ahau-Batz’’s face darkened. “Enough.”

Sirens cut the air.

Not sirens—drums. Deep, pulsing, slow.

The plaza beyond the scriptorium gates fell silent.

Then came the chant.

*“K’inich Ahau-T’ot’! The scribe who walks unseen!”*

K’inich’s stomach dropped.

Ahau-Batz’ shoved the girl toward the storage hut’s door. “Go. Hide. Now.”

The girl scrambled inside and vanished.

K’inich and Ahau-Batz’ stood frozen as the chants grew louder, spreading like fire.

Then the guards came.

Two of them, spears levelled, stepping through the gate.

K’inich turned to Ahau-Batz’. “Run.”

They bolted for the scriptorium’s inner courtyard, weaving between scribes, knocking over a pot of ink. Black liquid splashed across the stones.

The shouts behind them grew sharper.

K’inich’s lungs burned.

Ahead, the courtyard opened to sky. No cover. No escape.

Ahau-Batz’ grabbed his arm and yanked him sideways, into the shadow of a low storage alcove.

They ducked behind a pile of reed mats.

Bare feet slapped stone. Spears scraped.

“There he is!” a guard shouted.

K’inich’s heart hammered.

Ahau-Batz’ pressed close, his bandaged hand pressing against K’inich’s back. Warm. Alive.

From the shadows of the alcove, K’inich saw the guards pause, scanning the courtyard.

One pointed. “The quiet one. With the quill marks.”

K’inich’s fingers twitched. He had no quill. No mark.

The guards advanced.

Then—a scream from the market beyond.

High, clear, desperate.

*“The scribe who wasn’t there saved us once!”*

The guards’ heads snapped toward the sound.

K’inich and Ahau-Batz’ exhaled together.

The distraction gave them seconds. Just enough.

K’inich met Ahau-Batz’ eyes.

He nodded.

They ran.

Chapter 9

The scriptorium courtyard baked under the afternoon sun. Shards of light fractured through the fronds of the courtyard ceiba, casting shifting patterns on the gray stone where T’ot’ knelt, tracing the edges of a fallen quill with his thumb. The place smelled of damp bark and crushed herb—itzel’s workshop, not the grand halls where scribes drank cacao and debated eclipses.

A shadow fell across his work.

T’ot’ didn’t look up. He didn’t breathe.

“Scribe T’ot’,” said a voice like cut limestone. “You’re small for a man who carries the weight of the sky.”

Wak-K’in.

T’ot’ set the quill down. The courtyard was empty except for a bowl of half-dried ink and a single bark sheet rolled like a scroll. He wiped his hands on his loincloth, leaving black streaks.

“Itzel isn’t here,” T’ot’ said.

“I know.”

A pause. The air thickened.

Wak-K’in stepped closer. His sandals made no sound on the stone. He wore no jewelry, no insignia—only the authority of a man who had never doubted himself. Then he reached into the fold of his wrap and drew out something small and round.

A gold pyrite bead.

T’ot’ knew the symbol. Every scribe did. The bead was the mark of the royal bloodline, passed only through the king’s hand. Wak-K’in held it between thumb and forefinger, letting it catch the light.

“You have a skill,” Wak-K’in said. “One the city needs now more than ever.”

T’ot’ kept his hands still. “I fix what the sky forgets.”

“And the pyramid does not forget what the sky has already forgotten.” Wak-K’in rolled the bead between his fingers. “The Twin Pyramid’s foundation stones shift with the rain. The mortar is weak. The alignment is wrong.”

T’ot’ said nothing.

Wak-K’in leaned slightly forward. “I want you to correct it.”

A breeze stirred the ceiba leaves. T’ot’ heard the distant murmur of scribes in the scriptorium, the scratch of quills, the creak of reed pens against bark.

“Why me?” T’ot’ asked.

“Because you see what others miss.” Wak-K’in extended his hand, palm up. The bead gleamed. “I’ll give you back your name. No more whispers. No more hiding. You’ll be K’inich Ahau-T’ot’ again—not just the scribe who fixes what should not break.”

T’ot’’s fingers curled into fists. He felt the old ache in his bones, the one that came when he remembered Chan-Bahlum’s face in the firelight, the final push that sent him into the night.

He looked at the bead.

Then he reached out.

His fingers closed around it. Cold. Heavy. Alive.

Wak-K’in smiled. A thin, satisfied curve.

T’ot’ pocketed the bead.

The High Architect straightened, his shadow shrinking as he stepped back. “Bring the corrections to the northern workshop by dawn. The priests will install them before the next moonrise.”

T’ot’ nodded. One dip of the head. Enough.

Wak-K’in turned. His sandals did not whisper this time. They struck the stone like a verdict.

T’ot’ waited until the courtyard gate creaked shut behind the High Architect. Then he reached into his pocket.

The bead was warm from his body.

He rolled it across his palm, felt its weight, its edge biting into his skin. Then he stepped forward and placed it carefully on the ground.

He pressed it once with his sandal.

Then again.

The gold cracked into a dull gash on the courtyard stone. The edge curled inward, leaving a shallow dent.

T’ot’ rolled it with the ball of his foot toward the gutter at the edge of the courtyard.

The gutter was narrow. The bead hesitated at the lip.

He nudged it with his toe.

It fell.

A soft *clink* as it hit the stone below.

T’ot’ watched it disappear into the shadowed trough where rainwater drained from the scriptorium roof. It would lodge somewhere unseen, forgotten by everyone except the ants and the mold.

He wiped his foot on the edge of his wrap, leaving a smear of gold dust on the stone.

Then he walked to the edge of the courtyard where the reeds grew thickest and knelt in the damp earth.

He pressed his palm down where the bead had rested.

Left it there.

No words. No regret.

Just the shape of a footprint in the wet soil, already darkening at the edges.

Footsteps.

T’ot’ turned.

Ahau-Batz’ stood at the door of Itzel’s workshop, his arms crossed, his expression unreadable. A fresh bandage wrapped his hand, still smelling of herbs. He held a rolled bark sheet under one arm.

T’ot’ stood slowly. “You saw.”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t answer right away. He stepped into the courtyard, his sandals kicking up dust. “I saw the invitation.”

T’ot’ looked at the ground. The footprint. The faint gold dust.

“It’s bait,” he said.

“I know.”

“He wants me to fix his pyramid.”

“And claim credit for saving it.”

T’ot’ shook his head. “He wants me to admit I’m not just a scribe.”

Ahau-Batz’ came closer. Close enough that T’ot’ felt the heat of him. “You’re more than that already.”

T’ot’ didn’t answer.

Ahau-Batz’ unrolled the bark sheet with one hand. Star charts. Glyphs. Numbers. “I found something in the vault. Pages you hid. Pages that show the pyramid was never meant to hold the weight of the next katun.”

T’ot’ looked at the charts. His charts. The ones he’d copied from memory after the coup.

“Wak-K’in’s design is flawed,” Ahau-Batz’ said. “Not just the math. The foundation stones are laid on sand. The lintels are upside down in his sketches. He inverted the solstice angles.”

T’ot’ exhaled. “He knew.”

“No.” Ahau-Batz’ shook his head. “He thinks the errors are accidental. He doesn’t realize they were planted.”

A gust of wind rattled the ceiba leaves. T’ot’ felt the weight of old lies settle in his chest.

“He offered me the bead,” T’ot’ said quietly.

Ahau-Batz’ eyes flicked to his pocket—empty now. “Did you take it?”

“I rolled it into the gutter.”

Ahau-Batz’ was silent for a long moment. Then he nodded, once. “Good.”

T’ot’ looked at him. “Why are you here?”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t flinch. “Because you’re going to need more than a footprint to bury this.”

T’ot’ almost smiled. Almost.

But then Ahau-Batz’ gaze shifted behind him.

T’ot’ turned.

A shadow stretched across the courtyard floor—not from the ceiba, not from the sun.

It moved like a man stepping from darkness.

But the man wasn’t carrying a quill.

Or a bark sheet.

Or any tool of memory.

He carried a blade.

The man was tall. His cloak was dark, his face hidden beneath a low hood. He stood just inside the courtyard gate, one hand resting on the hilt of his knife.

T’ot’’s pulse quickened.

Ahau-Batz’ stepped forward, blocking T’ot’s view. “Who are you?”

The man didn’t answer. He shifted slightly, adjusting his grip.

Then he drew the blade.

Cold metal glinted in the courtyard light.

And he smiled.

Not at them. Not at the courtyard. Not at anything in the world they knew.

He smiled at the blade itself.

As if it had whispered something only he could hear.

Ahau-Batz’ shifted his weight, feet apart.

T’ot’ felt the old training rise in him—the same instinct that had once saved his life in the dark.

He didn’t wait.

He lunged for the reed pile.

Chapter 10

The vault of the Royal Scriptorium was a chamber of silence, its walls lined with cedar beams blackened by centuries of cedar smoke and incense. The air smelled of old paper, crushed copal, and the faint metallic tang of quill points. T’ot’ knelt on the stone floor, his knees pressing into the cold, his fingers splayed over the unrolled bark sheet. The *Tzolk’in Haab’* lay between him and Ahau-Batz’, its painted glyphs glowing under the flicker of a single obsidian lamp.

Ahau-Batz’ leaned over the chart, his shadow cutting across the lines of calculation. He traced a finger along the edge of a glyph, then paused. “This isn’t just a mistake. It’s sabotage.”

T’ot’ didn’t answer at first. He exhaled through his nose, felt the tension in his shoulders ease slightly. “Show me.”

Ahau-Batz’ unrolled a second sheet—a fragment of the Twin Pyramid’s inscribed calendar, stolen from the public plaza by a scribe who owed him a favor. The glyphs were larger, meant to be seen from a distance, carved into limestone with the precision of a man who believed his own legend.

T’ot’ compared them. The dates matched the sky. The seasons aligned. But the alignments were wrong.

“Here,” Ahau-Batz’ said, tapping the bark. “The solstice marker. It’s shifted three full days backward.”

T’ot’ followed the line. The error was deliberate. The glyphs weren’t just misplaced—they were inverted. The sun’s position was reversed, the moon’s cycle flipped. A scribe’s nightmare. A ruler’s disaster.

“He’s building a monument to a sky that never was,” Ahau-Batz’ said quietly.

T’ot’ didn’t speak. He didn’t need to. They both knew what this meant. Wak-K’in wasn’t just wrong. He was creating a false history—one that would collapse when the real sky refused to obey.

Ahau-Batz’ leaned back on his heels. “The foundation stones are laid on sand. The lintels are upside down. The entire pyramid is designed to fail unless someone corrects it.”

“And he wants me to do it,” T’ot’ said.

“He wants you to take credit for fixing what he broke.”

T’ot’ rolled the bark sheet carefully, flattening the creases. He set it aside. Then he pulled another bundle from beneath the obsidian lamp—a smaller, secret text, its edges singed. The handwriting was different. Not his own.

Ahau-Batz’ recognized it instantly. “The Bloodline Obelisk.”

T’ot’ nodded. “The hidden chamber. The one no one’s supposed to know about.”

He unrolled it. The glyphs were ancient, carved before the coup, before the fire. The royal lineage, traced back through generations. And at the bottom, in smaller, crisper strokes—his name.

K’inich Ahau-T’ot’.

Not as a memory. Not as a ghost. As a living heir.

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t touch it. He didn’t need to. The truth was written in the spacing between the glyphs—the careful omission of Chan-Bahlum’s death, the false declaration of T’ot’’s execution.

“He thought he erased you,” Ahau-Batz’ said. “But he only erased the wrong brother.”

T’ot’ felt the weight settle in his chest. Not pride. Not relief. A kind of cold clarity. The coup hadn’t been about power. It had been about memory. Wak-K’in had needed to erase the past to rewrite the future.

And he had used T’ot’ himself to do it.

Ahau-Batz’ sat back, his breath steady. “You gave him the bead.”

T’ot’ looked at his palm, empty now. “I gave it back.”

“You rolled it into the gutter.”

“It was never mine to hold.”

Ahau-Batz’ studied him. “You’re not just rejecting the title. You’re rejecting the whole game.”

T’ot’ didn’t answer. He rerolled the obelisk glyphs with deliberate care, the edges clicking as he fastened the ties.

“You realize what this means,” Ahau-Batz’ said. “If Wak-K’in finds out you have this—if anyone finds out—”

“Then he wins,” T’ot’ said. “He gets his monument. His false sky. His lie carved in stone forever.”

Ahau-Batz’ exhaled through his nose. “So we burn it.”

T’ot’ shook his head. “No.”

“You want to use it.”

“I want to expose it.”

A pause. The lamp flickered. Shadows danced along the cedar beams.

Ahau-Batz’ finally spoke, voice low. “You’re not just a scribe anymore, are you?”

T’ot’ met his eyes. “I never was.”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t flinch. He reached out and took the obelisk bundle from T’ot’s hands. He untied it again, just enough to see the glyphs. Then he traced the line of T’ot’’s name with his fingertip.

“Chan-Bahlum died saving you,” Ahau-Batz’ said.

T’ot’ said nothing.

“He didn’t die so you could hide.”

T’ot’ clenched his jaw. “He died so I could live.”

Ahau-Batz’ looked up. “And now you’re going to make his death mean something?”

T’ot’ took the bundle back. Rolled it tight. Tucked it into the fold of his loincloth.

“Yes.”

Ahau-Batz’ studied him for a long moment. Then he nodded, once. “Then you’ll need more than a hidden chamber.”

T’ot’ turned toward the vault’s narrow stairs. The air was cooler here, the stone damp with seepage from the cenote below. He could hear the distant hum of scribes in the main chamber, the scratch of reed pens on bark, the murmur of prayers to the gods of ink and memory.

Ahau-Batz’ fell into step beside him as they climbed. “You’re going public.”

T’ot’ didn’t answer.

“You’re going to stand in the plaza and say, *I am K’inich Ahau-T’ot’.*”

T’ot’ paused at the top of the stairs. The scriptorium’s main hall stretched before them, a forest of pillars and flickering lamps. Scribes bent over their work, oblivious.

“Not with words,” T’ot’ said. “With the sky.”

Ahau-Batz’ almost smiled. “An eclipse.”

“A corrected one.”

“And Wak-K’in?”

T’ot’ stepped into the hall. The air smelled of copal and damp bark. “He’ll try to stop it.”

“He’ll try to kill you.”

T’ot’ walked toward the east exit, where the corridor led to the palace gardens. Ahau-Batz’ kept pace.

“Then he’ll fail,” T’ot’ said.

Ahau-Batz’ glanced at him. “You’re sure?”

T’ot’ didn’t slow. “Because I’ve been dead for ten years. And the world didn’t notice.”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t answer. They reached the corridor. The exit was just ahead, a rectangle of sunlight cutting through the gloom.

Then a blade scraped on stone.

Not from the scriptorium. Not from the courtyard.

From the corridor’s mouth.

A guard stood there, his spear braced against the wall. His cloak was dark, his face hidden beneath a low hood. He held a knife—not a spear—in his hand.

Not a guard.

A spy.

And he wasn’t alone.

Three more figures emerged from the shadows behind him. All cloaked. All armed.

The lead spy smiled.

His teeth were filed to points.

Chapter 11

The Black Cenote exhaled mist. T’ot’ stood at the water’s edge, the torchlight carving his shadow long across the damp stone. Ixchel’s basket lay at his feet, its lid slightly askew. Inside, the Bloodline Tapestry glowed faintly—a shimmer of red thread against dark bark.

Ahau-Batz’ arrived without sound. He paused behind T’ot’, close enough to feel the heat of him, far enough to keep things sharp.

“You’re sure this is where she went?”

T’ot’ didn’t turn. “She left no mark. But this is where she would come.”

A drop of water fell from the vaulted ceiling. It hissed in the torch flame.

Ahau-Batz’ stepped forward, peered into the dark water. The cenote’s surface was still, reflecting nothing. “If Wak-K’in finds her with that tapestry—”

“Then the truth dies before it’s born,” T’ot’ finished.

He knelt, lifted the basket. The tapestry shifted as he moved it, the threads whispering like a hushed prayer. He rewrapped it tight, securing the lid with a strip of amate.

Ahau-Batz’ crouched beside him. “You think Ti-Hu took it?”

“I think she couldn’t bear to see it burned.”

“Or she wants to sell it to Calakmul.”

T’ot’ fastened the last knot. “Lady Ti-Hu doesn’t sell. She poisons.”

A gust of damp air rushed up from below. It carried the scent of old sacrifice and rotting reeds.

Then voices.

Not from the cenote’s entrance. From above.

Shouts. Running feet. The clatter of sandals on stone.

Ahau-Batz’ stood fast. “Guards.”

T’ot’ rose, basket in hand. “Not just guards.”

He recognized the stride—the measured step of Wak-K’in’s elite cadre, the ones who moved like shadows with polished stone in their hands.

Ahau-Batz’ turned toward the ladder. “We go up.”

T’ot’ didn’t move. “They’ll see the light. They’ll drop.”

Ahau-Batz’ assessed the cenote’s ceiling—loose stone, crumbling edges. “Then we go down.”

T’ot’ looked at the water. The dark was absolute. The current unknown. But the current wasn’t the danger.

The voices were closer.

Ahau-Batz’ grabbed his wrist. “Trust the water.”

T’ot’ stepped in. The surface parted with a sigh. The cold bit deep, through loincloth and skin. He sank fast, the tapestry bundle dragging at his waist. The torchlight above shrank to a pinprick.

Ahau-Batz’ followed, his breath sharp in the dark.

They descended into the silence.

---

Ixchel moved through the palace corridors like a ghost in a red mantle. The Bloodline Tapestry was rolled tight beneath her arm, the royal glyphs hidden from prying eyes. She didn’t look back. Not once.

Walls passed. Torches sputtered. A eunuch’s shadow flickered across a fresco of gods.

Then a hand clamped over her mouth.

She twisted. The tapestry slipped. Her elbow struck bone. A cry died in her throat.

“You drop that,” a voice hissed in her ear, “and I drop you.”

Ixchel stilled. The scent of copal and iron filled her nose.

Lady Ti-Hu’s fingers tightened. “Where is he?”

Ixchel said nothing.

Ti-Hu leaned closer. “Where is the prince?”

Nothing.

A guard’s spear tip appeared in the corridor’s mouth, glinting in torchlight. Then another.

Ti-Hu exhaled through her nose. “You’re afraid of him. That’s why you hide him.”

Ixchel’s eyes flicked toward the shadows near the jade mosaic of the Waterlily Jaguar.

Ti-Hu’s grip loosened slightly. “He’s not worth your life.”

Ixchel said nothing.

Ti-Hu released her. “Fine. But remember—when Wak-K’in’s men find you, I won’t be the one to beg for your mercy.”

She turned on her heel, cloak swirling like blood in water.

Ixchel watched her go. Then she turned and ran—not toward the exit, but deeper into the palace, toward the abandoned ritual chambers where the scribes once calculated the stars before the coup.

The tapestry unrolled in her grip as she ran. The royal thread glinted in the torchlight. The name of a dead prince shone in the dark.

K’inich Ahau-T’ot’.

Alive.

Real.

She skidded into the chamber, breath ragged. The walls were carved with old eclipse tables, the stone worn smooth by prayers. She pressed the tapestry against the farthest glyph—a hidden niche no one had used in years.

She pushed.

The stone shifted. A seam opened.

She ducked inside. The hidden chamber was cold. Empty. Perfect.

She sealed herself in.

Then she heard it.

A shout. From the corridor.

Then boots. Many boots.

Her breath stopped.

They were coming.

---

Wak-K’in stood in the center of his private chamber, his hands clasped behind his back. The air smelled of copal and burnt cedar. A low table held a half-empty cup of balché, its surface slick with condensation.

He had expected Ti-Hu by now.

She was late.

A guard entered, head bowed. “High Architect, the palace is in motion. Scribes are sealing their scrolls. Sentinels double their patrols.”

Wak-K’in didn’t turn. “And the scribe called T’ot’?”

“Gone from the scriptorium. The vault is empty.”

Wak-K’in exhaled slowly through his nose. “Find him.”

The guard hesitated. “There is more.”

Wak-K’in finally turned. “Speak.”

“The obelisk. The Bloodline Obelisk. It’s been disturbed.”

Wak-K’in’s fingers twitched. “Disturbed how?”

“Stone moved. Dust on the floor. As if someone knelt there. Long enough to read.”

Wak-K’in walked to the obsidian mirror on his wall. He studied his reflection—the high forehead, the sharp cheekbones, the eyes that had planned the Twin Pyramid before the next katun cycle.

He smiled.

“Then we make sure the disturbance isn’t repeated.”

The guard bowed and left.

Wak-K’in poured another cup of balché. He drank it slowly, savoring the bitterness.

The scribes would resist. The priests would hesitate. But the sky would obey.

The Twin Pyramid would rise.

And when the next eclipse came, the people would see.

Not the truth.

A monument.

---

The Bloodline Obelisk chamber smelled of old stone and forgotten prayers. T’ot’ stood before the defaced slab, his fingers tracing the cracks in the glyphs. The hidden chamber had been opened before—by someone cautious, methodical. Not Ixchel.

Then his palm brushed a loose chip of limestone.

It fell.

Dust sifted into the dark below.

He knelt. The torchlight reached deep into a hidden crevice. His breath caught.

There, cut into the stone floor, fresh chisel marks. Three lines. A name.

K’inich.

Not erased. Not defaced.

Carved.

He reached in. His fingers brushed bark. A roll of amate, damp with cenote mist.

Then a shadow moved behind him.

He turned.

Wak-K’in stood in the chamber’s narrow doorway, torch in hand. His face was calm. His eyes were not.

“You,” he said.

T’ot’ didn’t rise. Didn’t speak.

Wak-K’in stepped forward. The torch flame bent toward him, then steadied.

“You took something from me,” Wak-K’in said. “The mantle of time. The mantle of memory. You stole it.”

T’ot’ stood slowly. The amate roll remained in his hand.

Wak-K’in’s gaze flicked to it. “What do you hold?”

T’ot’ didn’t answer.

Wak-K’in raised his free hand. From the shadows behind him, two guards emerged. Spears leveled.

“Put it down,” Wak-K’in said. “Or they run you through.”

T’ot’ looked at the roll. He could feel the royal glyphs beneath his fingers—K’inich Ahau-T’ot’, alive. Real. Unbroken.

He tightened his grip.

Wak-K’in’s smile faded.

Then—

A guard gasped.

The torch slipped from Wak-K’in’s grip.

It fell.

Stone cracked. Dust exploded.

The chamber’s exit—once sealed tight—shuddered. Mortar gave way.

The ceiling groaned.

Wak-K’in barely had time to shout before the stones came down.

chapters.title: **Chapter 12: The Crown in the Dust**
chapters.text:
**Chapter 12: The Crown in the Dust**

The ceiling screamed.

Dust plumed like a sacrificed quetzal’s wings, swirling in the torchlight before settling over everything—stone, bone, and the man who had once been an architect.

Wak-K’in coughed, waving a hand through the haze. His torch had guttered out. Only embers glowed in the dark, revealing the shape of his guards—one prone, the other clawing at his own leg, pinned beneath a slab of limestone.

T’ot’ didn’t wait. He lunged for the chamber’s entrance, but the stones there had shifted into a jagged barricade.

Wak-K’in’s voice cut through the chaos. “You think this changes anything?”

T’ot’ turned. The amate roll was still clenched in his fingers, its edges blackened by the fallen torch.

Wak-K’in stepped forward, his sandals crunching on shattered stucco. “You brought this down. Not me. The weight of a lie always collapses.”

T’ot’ said nothing. He pressed the amate roll against his chest.

Wak-K’in’s eyes flicked to it. “That? A scrap of bark. You clutch it like a royal decree.”

T’ot’ unrolled it just enough for the torchlight to catch the royal glyphs. The ink shimmered, wet and fresh.

Wak-K’in went still.

The name *K’inich Ahau-T’ot’* glared back at him.

The guards staggered upright, spears trembling. One muttered a prayer to Bolon-Tiku. The other lunged at T’ot’.

Wak-K’in held up a hand. “No.”

The guard froze.

Wak-K’in’s voice dropped. “Drop the scroll.”

T’ot’ didn’t move.

Wak-K’in’s fingers twitched toward the guard’s spear. “That thing was never yours to carry. That name was never yours to claim.”

T’ot’ exhaled through his nose. “It’s not a name. It’s the sky.”

Wak-K’in’s jaw tightened. “The sky bows to the stone we build. To the time we carve into it. That’s power. That’s legacy.”

T’ot’ rolled the scroll tighter. “Legacy is what’s left when the stone cracks.”

A guard whimpered. Wak-K’in glared at him, then back at T’ot’.

“You think the scribes will rally behind you?” Wak-K’in laughed, low and rough. “They serve the calendar. The calendar serves me.”

T’ot’ shook his head. “The calendar is broken.”

Wak-K’in’s smile vanished. “Then fix it.”

Silence.

Dust sifted from the ceiling. Somewhere above, voices rose—shouts, running feet. The royal guards were coming.

Wak-K’in stepped closer. “You want the throne? Take it. But know this—when the next eclipse comes, the people will see the sky follow my calculations, not yours. And they will tear you apart for your arrogance.”

T’ot’ didn’t flinch. “The sky doesn’t follow men. Men follow the sky.”

Wak-K’in’s hand shot out. He grabbed T’ot’s wrist, twisting. The scroll slipped.

T’ot’ twisted free. The amate rolled across the stone floor, unspooling in the dim light.

The royal glyphs blazed.

Wak-K’in’s breath stopped.

K’inich Ahau-T’ot’.

The name of a dead prince.

A living prince.

The guards gasped. One dropped to his knees.
