Wak-K’in’s grip on his own chest tightened, as if the air had turned to stone. “You—”

“I’m the ghost you tried to bury,” T’ot’ said. “The prince you forgot.”

Wak-K’in’s lips peeled back. “You were erased.”

“You erased him,” T’ot’ said. “And you forgot to check the tomb.”

A guard scrambled toward the scroll. Wak-K’in kicked it away, sending it skittering into the dark.

T’ot’ lunged for it. Wak-K’in blocked him, shoulder to shoulder. They grappled, arms wrestling, breath hot and sharp.

The scroll lay half-unrolled, its edges glowing like embers in the dark.

Wak-K’in’s voice was a blade. “That changes nothing.”

T’ot’ shoved him back. “It changes everything.”

Wak-K’in wiped dust from his mouth. “The scribes will say you’re a fraud. The priests will say you’re cursed. The people will say you’re a liar.”

T’ot’ picked up the scroll. His fingers trembled only once.

“Let them,” he said.

Above them, the chamber shuddered. A crack split the ceiling, thin as a hair, then widened.

Wak-K’in looked up. “You brought this ruin.”

T’ot’ didn’t answer.

The ceiling groaned.

Then it fell.

Chapter 13

The Black Cenote swallowed the dawn.

Water lapped at the stone ledge where K’inich knelt, wrists bound behind him. The ropes bit, but he didn’t shift. Not yet. Not until he was sure no one else was near.

Lady Ti-Hu stood above him, her embroidered sandals pressed into the slick rock. She held a dagger—long, curved, the kind meant for bloodletting or necks. The blade caught the pale light filtering down through the cenote’s mouth.

“You should have run when you had the chance,” she said.

K’inich tilted his head back. The hole above was a circle of blue, too small to climb through unless you were a bat. “I didn’t have the chance. You blocked it.”

She crouched, the dagger hovering over his throat. The edge trembled—not from fear, but calculation. “I gave you every chance. A warning in the scriptorium. A night to disappear. Yet here you are, still digging up corpses.”

He exhaled. “Not corpses. just names.”

Ti-Hu’s fingers twitched. “Names that burn. Names that hang men.”

K’inich watched the water ripple. It hid the bones of spies and failed princes. “You wouldn’t be here if you thought I was just a scribe.”

“No,” she said softly. “But I wouldn’t be here if I thought you were a prince either.”

He almost laughed. “Then what do you think I am?”

She leaned closer. The scent of copal and burnt copal oil clung to her. “A man who thinks he can rewrite the sky.”

K’inich said nothing.

Ti-Hu pressed the dagger’s tip against his pulse. Not hard enough to draw blood. Just enough to make him feel the weight of it. “You’ve made a mistake, T’ot’. Spreading royal scrolls. naming yourself a ghost. Wak-K’in won’t kneel to a resurrected prince. He’ll bury you twice.”

K’inich flexed his fingers behind his back. The ropes were thick jute, old, fraying at the ends. “And you? What do you want?”

Ti-Hu’s smile was thin. “I want Tikal to burn from the inside. I want Calakmul to watch it crumble. I want the scribes to wake up and realize their ink bled dry long ago.”

The cenote walls seemed to lean in.

K’inich kept his voice steady. “And if I don’t give you that fire?”

She tapped the dagger against his cheekbone. “Then I’ll take it from your bones.”

A splash echoed from the tunnel behind her.

Ti-Hu’s head snapped around.

K’inich lunged.

The ropes snapped as he twisted, his shoulder crashing into her ribs. They hit the wet stone together, the dagger skittering into the dark. Ti-Hu gasped, rolled, scrambled toward the blade—but K’inich kicked out, his heel catching her wrist. Bone cracked.

She howled. The sound bounced off the cenote walls, raw and furious.

K’inich didn’t wait. He lunged past her, scrambling toward the tunnel mouth. The exit was narrow, slick with algae. He squeezed through, heart hammering.

Behind him, Ti-Hu screamed a curse in Yucatec.

He didn’t look back.

The tunnel twisted downward, then opened into a chamber lit by a single smoking copal censer. Ahau-Batz’ stood in the center, his arms crossed, his face half in shadow. A quill was tucked behind his ear, ink staining his fingers.

He didn’t move.

K’inich stumbled in, breath ragged. “You followed me?”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t answer. His eyes flicked past K’inich, toward the tunnel behind. Then back.

K’inich turned.

Ti-Hu stood there, one hand clutched to her ribs, the other holding the dagger—now red.

“You didn’t kill me,” she spat. “But you should have.”

Ahau-Batz’ stepped forward. “You’re bleeding on my floor.”

Ti-Hu’s laugh was a knife twist. “Your floor? Everything here is Tikal’s floor. And it’s burning.”

K’inich wiped sweat from his brow. The air was thick, humid. The censer’s smoke curled toward the ceiling, carrying the scent of burnt prayers.

Ahau-Batz’ tilted his head. “She’s right. You lit the fuse when you named yourself. Now the whole city’s counting down.”

Ti-Hu spat on the stone. “Counting down to ashes.”

K’inich ignored her. He focused on Ahau-Batz’. “Why did you come?”

Ahau-Batz’ exhaled through his nose. “Because I read the sky tonight. And it said you’d need a witness.”

K’inich’s pulse jumped. “The eclipse?”

“Not the eclipse.” Ahau-Batz’ stepped closer, his voice low. “The silence after.”

A shadow shifted in the tunnel.

Ti-Hu’s eyes flicked that way. “Oh, clever. You brought the court to you.”

Wak-K’in stepped into the light.

He was covered in dust and dried blood. His once-pristine jaguar pectoral was cracked across the chest. A gash ran from his temple to his jawline. He didn’t look like an architect. He looked like a man who’d clawed through ruin to get here.

Ti-Hu’s grip on the dagger tightened. “Lord Wak-K’in.”

He didn’t acknowledge her. His gaze locked onto K’inich.

“You took something from me,” Wak-K’in said. His voice was gravel and fury. “Not just a name. Not just a throne. You took my *city*. My *time*. My *name carved into the stars*.”

K’inich didn’t flinch. “You carved lies into the stars.”

Wak-K’in’s hands clenched. “You don’t understand what I built. The Twin Pyramid will stand when your bones are ash. The calendar will outlive your bloodline.”

Ahau-Batz’ spoke quietly. “And when it fails? When the next eclipse doesn’t align? When the people realize the sky doesn’t bow to your stone?”

Wak-K’in’s lips peeled back. “It won’t fail. I won’t let it.”

Ti-Hu laughed—a wet, pained sound. “You already did. K’inich corrected the prediction. The sky obeys the true codex, not your monument.”

Wak-K’in’s chest heaved. He swung toward K’inich. “Then you admit it. You *are* him. You *stole* the codex. You *faked* your survival.”

K’inich didn’t answer.

Wak-K’in took a step forward. “You think this changes anything? The scribes will burn your scrolls. The priests will call you a demon. The people will tear you apart for daring to rise.”

Ti-Hu wiped blood from her lip. “And yet here we are. The prince, the architect, the spy, and the traitor—all in one pit.”

Wak-K’in ignored her. His eyes burned into K’inich.

“You wanted the throne?” he said. “Take it. Stand in the plaza at dawn and call yourself king. Let the scribes see you. Let the priests denounce you. Let the people decide.”

K’inich said nothing.

Wak-K’in spread his arms. “But know this—when you fail, when the sky betrays you just like it did me, I will be there. And I will laugh while I light the pyre.”

Ahau-Batz’ stepped between them. “Enough.”

Wak-K’in didn’t move. “You always were sentimental, Ahau-Batz’. Just like your father.”

Ahau-Batz’ stiffened. “My father warned you. But you wouldn’t listen.”

Wak-K’in’s gaze flicked to Ti-Hu. “She listens. She feeds the fire.”

Ti-Hu wiped her dagger on her skirt. “I feed the truth. And the truth is—you lost.”

Wak-K’in’s shoulders slumped, just for a moment. Then he straightened.

“No,” he said. “I just haven’t burned it all down yet.”

He turned and walked back into the tunnel’s darkness. His footsteps echoed, then faded.

Ti-Hu watched him go. Then she looked at K’inich.

“This isn’t over,” she said. “The next eclipse isn’t just about time. It’s about power. And you don’t have enough of either.”

K’inich said nothing.

She tossed the dagger at his feet. It clattered against the stone.

“Next time, use it,” she said. “Before Wak-K’in does.”

Then she limped into the tunnel after him.

Silence settled.

Ahau-Batz’ exhaled. “He’s not wrong about one thing.”

K’inich picked up the dagger. The edge was still warm.

“About what?”

“You don’t have enough.” Ahau-Batz’ stepped closer, close enough that K’inich could feel the heat of him. “Not enough time. Not enough proof. Not enough people.”

K’inich turned the dagger over in his hand. “Then we get more.”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t flinch. “How?”

K’inich met his eyes. “We give them a reason to believe.”

Ahau-Batz’ was close enough to touch. Close enough that K’inich could see the flecks of gold in his dark pupils. Close enough that the heat between them wasn’t just from the censer’s smoke.

Ahau-Batz’ voice dropped. “A public ritual. A shared sky. A claim so loud the scribes can’t ignore it.”

K’inich didn’t look away. “And if it burns us both?”

Ahau-Batz’ smiled—a small, fierce thing. “Then we burn together.”

K’inich’s breath caught.

The censer sputtered.

Above them, in the cenote’s mouth, a shadow moved.

Not Wak-K’in.

Not Ti-Hu.

A child’s silhouette—small, silent, watching.

Then gone.

K’inich’s grip tightened on the dagger.

Ahau-Batz’ noticed. “What is it?”

K’inich said nothing. He didn’t take his eyes off the hole above.

But he knew.

Someone had seen.

Someone would tell.

And soon, Wak-K’in wouldn’t be the only one holding a torch.

Chapter 14

Dawn clawed over the Temple of the Giant Jaguar. Stone and stucco glowed amber in the rising light. K’inich stood in the shadow of the eastern staircase, his fingers tracing the jagged edge of the dagger still slick in his grip.

Ti-Hu lay at the base of the steps, wrists bound with her own sash, her embroidered skirt soaked red. She was alive. Barely.

He crouched beside her. “You want to live?”

She groaned, rolling onto her side. “I want to kill you.”

“Then you’re going to talk first.” He pressed the dagger’s tip against the tendons of her wrist. Not hard enough to draw blood. Just enough to make her flinch. “Tell me how Calakmul got into the scriptorium.”

Ti-Hu bared her teeth. “Go to the Black Cenote. Find the tapestry beneath the broken slab. Then ask me again.”

K’inich leaned closer. “I’m not asking again. I’m telling you to speak.”

She spat. The glob landed on his sandal. He didn’t react.

Above them, the plaza filled with scribes and porters. They moved in silence, heads down, carrying baskets of bark paper and obsidian blades. The Great Twin Pyramid loomed in the distance, its limestone face catching the sun like a blade.

K’inich kept his voice low. “You moved agents into the calendar guild. You left messages in the Bloodline Tapestry. You turned the scribes into your eyes and ears. And now you claim you didn’t order it?”

Ti-Hu’s laugh was a wet, broken thing. “You think I answer to Calakmul? They answer to *me*. I wanted Wak-K’in to see his walls crack. I wanted the scribes to doubt their own ink.”

K’inich’s grip tightened. “And the prince? The heir they erased?”

She stilled. “That wasn’t me.”

“No?” He pressed the dagger harder. “Then who forged the royal bead Wak-K’in gave me? Who put a stolen heirloom in the hand of a liar?”

Ti-Hu’s eyes flickered. Not fear. Recognition. “You have it?”

“It’s gone.” He pushed to his feet. “Burned.”

She exhaled, long and slow. “Then it’s done.”

“Not even close.” K’inich wiped the dagger on his thigh. “The bead was a message. To me. From someone who wanted me dead or on the throne. You’re telling me you didn’t send it?”

Ti-Hu’s lips curled. “If I wanted you dead, boy, you’d be feeding the cenote eels by now.”

Footsteps crunched on gravel. Ahau-Batz’ appeared at the top of the staircase, his robes damp with sweat, his quill tucked behind his ear. He took in the scene—Ti-Hu bound, K’inich holding the dagger—and his jaw tightened.

K’inich didn’t lower the blade. “She’s talking. Sort of.”

Ahau-Batz’ stepped down. “She’s bleeding on the temple steps. That’s not talking. That’s a death sentence.”

Ti-Hu’s eyes flicked to him. “Disgraced heir. Still loyal to the court that spat on your name.”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t answer. He looked at K’inich. “What do you want?”

“Answers.” K’inich kept the dagger steady. “The truth about Calakmul. The truth about the bead. The truth about why anyone would want me dead before I’m even crowned.”

Ti-Hu smirked. “You think you’re being crowned?”

K’inich didn’t react.

Ahau-Batz’ crossed his arms. “She’s not giving you anything real. She’ll spin until the last ember dies.”

Ti-Hu’s grin widened. “Spoken like a man who still believes in truth.”

Ahau-Batz’ stepped closer. “I believe in what the sky says. And it says you’re out of time.”

Ti-Hu’s breath hitched. Just for a second. A crack in her armor.

K’inich saw it. He crouched again, pressing the dagger to her throat—not to cut, but to make her listen. “Who gave Wak-K’in the bead?”

Her pulse jumped under the steel.

Ahau-Batz’ knelt beside K’inich. “It doesn’t matter. The bead is dust. The codex is real. The people will follow the one who made the sky obey.”

Ti-Hu’s voice was a rasp. “Wak-K’in stole the codex’s predictions. He changed the glyphs. He made the sky lie.”

K’inich didn’t react. “And you let him?”

She barked a laugh. “I *made* him. I fed him false data. I let him build his monument of sand. Because when it crumbles—and it *will*—the people will see he’s a fraud. And then *you* walk in, the forgotten prince, to save them all.”

Ahau-Batz’ stiffened. “You manipulated Wak-K’in into failure so K’inich could—”

“—take the throne without a drop of blood.” Ti-Hu’s eyes gleamed. “Yes.”

Silence settled, thick and uneasy.

K’inich lowered the dagger. Just a fraction. “Why?”

Ti-Hu’s voice dropped. “Because Tikal deserves better than a king who carves lies into the stars.”

Ahau-Batz’ exhaled. “And you? What do *you* deserve?”

She looked at him. Really looked. “To watch the man who burned my city choke on his own monument.”

K’inich stood. The dagger hung at his side. “You’ll talk more. In the Black Cenote. With the knife at your throat or the truth in your mouth. Your choice.”

Ti-Hu’s eyes blazed. “I choose fire.”

Ahau-Batz’ stepped between them. “Enough games. If she’s telling the truth, then Wak-K’in is already building his pyre. We need to move.”

K’inich nodded. He turned toward the plaza.

Then he stopped.

Ahau-Batz’ followed his gaze.

Across the square, beyond the scribes and the Twin Pyramid’s shadow, a crowd had gathered at the base of the Temple of the Mask. A man stood on the steps, his arms raised. The sun caught the gold in his dark hair, the jade at his throat.

Ahau-Batz’ inhaled. “What is he doing?”

K’inich didn’t answer.

Ahau-Batz’ started forward. “That’s reckless. That’s—”

K’inich grabbed his arm. “Wait.”

The crowd parted. Someone laughed. A child pointed.

Ahau-Batz’ froze.

K’inich’s fingers tightened.

Ahau-Batz’ was standing in the open now, his robes catching the light, his disgraced name whispered in the crowd. He wasn’t hiding. He wasn’t cowering.

He was declaring.

“People of Tikal,” Ahau-Batz’ called, his voice carrying over the plaza. “I stand with the last prince of the Ajaw Kan bloodline. I stand with K’inich Ahau-T’ot’!”

A ripple ran through the crowd.

K’inich’s throat went dry.

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t stop. “I stand against the liar who carved his name into the sky! I stand against the architect who built his power on sand!”

Scribes in the plaza looked up. Porters dropped their burdens. A priest at the Twin Pyramid’s base turned, his face a mask of shock.

Ahau-Batz’ raised his arms higher. “I choose the man who made the stars obey his truth!”

A murmur rolled through the square. Then a gasp. Then a shout.

K’inich stepped forward, his pulse a drum in his ears.

Ahau-Batz’ locked eyes with him across the plaza. Held.

Then he dropped to one knee.

In the center of the plaza, in front of everyone, Ahau-Batz’ pressed his palms to the stone. “I kneel to the prince who was never erased. I kneel to the heir who was never dead.”

Murmurs turned to shouts. Some were anger. Some were awe.

K’inich’s hands trembled.

Above the plaza, on the western stair of the Twin Pyramid, Wak-K’in stood like a carved god. His face was a ruin of dust and blood, his jaguar pectoral split down the middle.

He roared.

The sound shook the stones.

“I’ll burn the city before I kneel!”

A torch flared to life in his hand.

The first flame licked the air.

K’inich watched the fire take hold.

The plaza erupted.

Chapter 15

The temple steps ran red.

K’inich moved through the blood, his sandals sticking to the stone. Ahau-Batz’ stayed close, his knuckles white around the obsidian blade they’d taken from Ti-Hu’s boot.

Wak-K’in didn’t run.

He stood atop the Twin Pyramid’s western stair, the fire in his hand eating the morning air. His robes fluttered like charred wings. His face was a ruin of ash and rage.

K’inich didn’t call out.

He didn’t need to.

The crowd between them was thinning, scuttling away like ants from a kicked mound. Some ran toward the cenote. Others toward the palace. A few stayed, frozen, watching.

Ahau-Batz’ gripped K’inich’s shoulder. “He’ll throw the torch.”

K’inich didn’t answer.

Wak-K’in’s voice cracked across the plaza. “You kneel to a ghost? To a forgery? To *him*?”

K’inich climbed.

Each step was a heartbeat. Each breath a countdown.

Ahau-Batz’ followed, silent.

Wak-K’in’s torch flared. “You think a name and a mask make you king?”

K’inich reached the last step.

Wak-K’in’s free hand went to the jade mask at his belt. He tore it off. Held it up. A jaguar’s maw, cracked down the middle.

“This is the blood of Tikal!” he shouted. “This is the face of the city!”

K’inich kept walking.

Wak-K’in’s eyes locked onto Ahau-Batz’. “You! Disgraced worm! You’d kneel to a scribe’s lie?”

Ahau-Batz’ didn’t flinch. “I kneel to the man who made the eclipse obey his hand.”

Wak-K’in snarled. “And when the sky betrays him? When the next eclipse comes and the calendar fails? When the people see *his* sky is as hollow as yours?”

K’inich stopped ten paces from the top.

Wak-K’in’s chest heaved. The torch’s light danced in his pupils.

K’inich spoke.

“You carved your name into the stars,” he said. “I made them answer.”

A ripple of silence.

Wak-K’in’s fingers twitched on the torch. “The bead.”

K’inich said nothing.

Wak-K’in’s voice dropped. “The royal bead. The one I gave you in the scriptorium. The one with Chan-Bahlum’s glyph.”

K’inich waited.

Wak-K’in’s face twisted. “You burned it.”

K’inich let the silence stretch.

Then he reached into the pouch at his belt. Pulled out a single jade bead.

Wak-K’in’s breath hitched.

K’inich rolled it between his fingers. Green glass in the torchlight.

Wak-K’in’s torch wavered. “That’s impossible.”

K’inich climbed the last step.

Wak-K’in raised the torch higher. “I watched you burn it.”

K’inich stopped a pace away.

Wak-K’in’s voice was a blade. “Show me.”

K’inich held out his hand.

The bead sat in his palm, catching the firelight.

Wak-K’in didn’t reach for it. His eyes were locked on the bead. His jaw worked.

Ahau-Batz’ shifted. “K’inich…”

Wak-K’in’s voice was raw. “Chan-Bahlum’s glyph.”

K’inich didn’t speak.

Wak-K’in’s torch trembled. “That bead was a message. A warning. A test.”

K’inich closed his fingers around the bead.

Wak-K’in’s eyes flicked up. “You were supposed to die with it.”

K’inich said nothing.

Wak-K’in’s face went slack. “You *know*.”

The plaza held its breath.

K’inich stepped forward.

Wak-K’in didn’t move back.

The torch cracked in Wak-K’in’s grip. A spark landed on his sleeve. He didn’t notice.

K’inich reached out.

His fingers brushed Wak-K’in’s wrist.

Wak-K’in flinched.

K’inich closed his hand around Wak-K’in’s wrist and squeezed.

The torch fell.

It hit the stone steps and died.

Darkness rushed in.

Wak-K’in didn’t pull away.

K’inich leaned in. His voice was quiet.

“You gave me the bead,” he said. “Not as a gift. As a test. You wanted me to fail it.”

Wak-K’in’s breath was hot on K’inich’s face.

K’inich tightened his grip. “You wanted to see if the last prince of Tikal would burn his own blood.”

Wak-K’in’s eyes burned.

K’inich’s voice dropped. “You wanted to know if I was still weak.”

A child’s voice piped up from the steps below.

“Señor.”

Both men turned.

A girl—no older than eight—stood at the base of the stair. Her dress was patched, her feet bare. In her hand, a single jade bead.

Identical to the one in K’inich’s grip.

It glowed in the morning light.

K’inich’s breath stopped.

Wak-K’in’s grip on his wrist loosened.

The child climbed the steps. Slow. Deliberate.

K’inich didn’t move.

The bead in the girl’s hand caught the light. Perfect. Unbroken. A replica of Chan-Bahlum’s glyph.

Wak-K’in’s voice was a rasp. “Where did you get that?”

The girl didn’t look at him. She climbed until she stood even with K’inich.

She held out the bead.

K’inich stared at it.

The plaza below them had gone still.

The girl’s voice was clear.

“For the prince who never burned his brother’s mark.”

Silence.

Then Ahau-Batz’ exhaled. One word.

“Shit.”

The bead landed in K’inich’s palm.

Wak-K’in’s breath came fast.

K’inich closed his fingers around it.

The girl’s eyes met his.

Then she turned and ran down the steps.

K’inich watched her go.

The plaza erupted.

Scribes shouted. Citizens pointed. Priests raised their hands.

Wak-K’in didn’t move.

K’inich flexed his fingers around the bead.

The jade was cold.

The plaza was chaos.

And for the first time in his life, K’inich Ahau-T’ot’ sat on the Ajaw throne.
